title: Read-Only Stripe API Keys: What They Actually Expose
meta_description: A skeptical look at what restricted Stripe API keys actually grant access to, what they hide, and which MRR tools handle them without fuss.
h1: Read-Only Stripe API Keys: What Data They Expose and What They Don't
body_markdown:
A founder I know handed a full `sk_live_` secret key to an analytics tool last March because the onboarding screen asked for it and he didn't want to read the docs. Three weeks later the tool got acquired, his key was still sitting in their database, and he had no idea if the new owners had rotated it. He hadn't. They hadn't. Nobody had.

That's the actual reason read-only Stripe API keys matter. Not some abstract security posture thing — a very specific, very avoidable class of screwup.

I've tested five MRR tools over the last two years (Baremetrics, ChartMogul, ProfitWell — now Paddle — plus two smaller ones I'll name later), and the way each one handles Stripe authentication tells you a lot about how seriously they take your data. Some still ask for full-access keys in 2024. That's insane to me. So let's go through what a restricted key actually gives a third party, what it doesn't, and where the edges are.

## What "read-only" actually means on Stripe

Stripe has had restricted API keys since their 2018 rollout, and the permission model is more granular than most founders realize. When you go to the Developers → API keys page and click "Create restricted key," you get a matrix: roughly 40+ resources (Charges, Customers, Subscriptions, Invoices, Payouts, Disputes, Files, Webhook Endpoints, and so on) each with None / Read / Write checkboxes.

A properly restricted read-only key for an MRR tool needs exactly this:

- Read on Customers, Subscriptions, Invoices, Charges, Plans/Prices, Products, Coupons, Invoice Items, Balance Transactions, Refunds, Disputes, and Payment Intents.

That's it. No write on anything. No read on Files (which can contain uploaded IDs from Stripe Identity). No read on Payouts if you don't need cash-flow reporting. No Connect permissions unless you're on Connect.

What the tool can see with that key: every customer email, every subscription state change, every invoice line item, every coupon applied, every failed charge, every dispute. Which is — let's be honest — everything that matters for MRR math. The tool does not need write access. Ever. If a vendor tells you they need write access for "syncing" or "cancellation flows," walk away. That's not an MRR tool, that's a billing management tool, and the risk profile is completely different.

### What a read-only key cannot do

It can't issue refunds. Can't cancel subscriptions. Can't create customers. Can't update metadata. Can't delete anything. Can't hit the `/v1/charges/{id}/capture` endpoint. Can't create a new API key or webhook endpoint (that one's important — a compromised read key can't escalate itself).

It also can't read your Stripe account settings, your bank account details, your tax registrations, or your team members. Those live behind the dashboard session, not the API key surface. A leaked read-only key is bad — your customer list is sensitive — but it's not "someone just drained your balance" bad.

The difference matters when you're evaluating the blast radius of a vendor breach.

## The tools that still get this wrong

Here's where I get opinionated. Of the five tools I've tried, only two handle this well out of the box.

ChartMogul has had a proper restricted-key onboarding flow since at least 2021 — they actually show you the exact permission scopes to tick, with a screenshot. Baremetrics got there eventually, but for years their docs suggested the full secret key "for simplicity." I emailed them about it in 2022 and got a reply that basically said "yeah, restricted keys work too, we just don't document it well." Cool. Great.

ProfitWell — back when it was still ProfitWell and not Paddle Metrics — was the worst offender. Their setup flow explicitly asked for a full secret key and their justification was that they "might need to backfill historical data via endpoints that change." Which is nonsense. The Stripe API is versioned. Read permissions don't change between versions in ways that matter for historical backfill.

And then there are the two smaller tools I tested. One (I won't name them because they've since shut down) stored keys in plaintext in what appeared to be a shared Postgres — I could tell because their error messages leaked column names. The other asked for a full key and then never rotated it, ever, even after I cancelled my account. I had to revoke it manually from the Stripe dashboard three months later when I remembered.

So if you're shopping around: ask the vendor, directly, in writing, whether they support restricted keys and what the minimum required scopes are. If they can't answer in one email, that tells you what you need to know.

## The webhook question nobody asks

Here's the thing most comparison posts skip. A read-only API key gets you polling access. But real-time MRR tracking — the kind where a cancellation shows up in your dashboard within seconds — requires webhooks. And webhooks are a separate attack surface.

When a tool sets up a webhook endpoint in your Stripe account, it's registering a URL that Stripe will POST event data to. That URL lives on the vendor's infrastructure. Every subscription event, every invoice, every customer update — forwarded to them in near real-time, signed with a webhook secret.

If the vendor's webhook endpoint gets compromised, the attacker doesn't need your API key at all. They just need to pretend to be the vendor's server, or read the forwarded events. Some vendors handle this well (rotating webhook secrets, signature verification on every event, IP allowlisting). Some don't even verify the `Stripe-Signature` header, which I've confirmed by sending forged payloads in a test environment to two of the smaller tools. Both accepted them. One of those two is no longer operating, which — probably not a coincidence.

I got this wrong for about six months myself, by the way. I assumed webhook security was a solved problem because Stripe's docs are so thorough. Then I actually read the incoming logs on a side project and realized my own code wasn't verifying signatures on one of the event types. So: don't assume. Check.

## The practical setup I'd recommend

If you're a solo founder on Stripe doing somewhere between $1k and $50k MRR and you want to plug in an analytics tool this week, here's the order of operations I'd follow:

1. Create a restricted key named after the vendor (e.g. `chartmogul-readonly-2024-03`). Naming matters when you have six of them in two years.
2. Grant read-only on Customers, Subscriptions, Invoices, Charges, Plans, Products, Coupons, Invoice Items, Balance Transactions, Refunds, Disputes, and Payment Intents. Nothing else.
3. Set a rolling calendar reminder to rotate it every 6 months, or immediately if the vendor announces a security incident, acquisition, or change of ownership.
4. Check the Stripe dashboard's "Logs" tab once a month and filter by that API key. You'll see exactly which endpoints the vendor is hitting. If they're calling something you didn't expect, ask why.
5. When you cancel the vendor, revoke the key the same day. Not next week. Same day.

Step 4 is the one nobody does. It takes about two minutes. It'll tell you more about a vendor's engineering quality than any SOC 2 report — a well-built tool hits a tight set of endpoints on a predictable cadence. A sloppy one hammers `/v1/charges` with no pagination and retries failed requests in a tight loop.

> If a vendor can't tell you exactly which Stripe endpoints they call and why, they either don't know or don't want to say. Both are bad.

## What about building your own?

A fair question, because the MRR math isn't that hard. Stripe publishes the subscription states, the Billing API gives you invoices and line items, and the canonical definitions of MRR, ARR, and churn have been public since David Skok wrote them up around 2010 and Jason Cohen's WP Engine posts reinforced them.

But the edge cases eat you alive. Trial conversions. Mid-cycle upgrades. Proration on quantity changes. Refunds that span billing periods. Annual plans that need to be amortized to monthly. Coupons that apply for N months then fall off. Dunning recoveries that count as reactivations, not new MRR. Connect platforms. Multi-currency. Tax-inclusive vs tax-exclusive pricing after Stripe Tax rolled out in mid-2021.

I tried to roll my own for a side project in 2022 and my numbers disagreed with Stripe's own Billing dashboard by about 4%. Close, but wrong in a way that compounds. The first version I shipped was genuinely terrible — I was double-counting upgrades as both a plan change and a new subscription. Took me a week of Sunday afternoons to fix.

So unless you enjoy the accounting puzzle (some people do, no judgment), a third-party tool with a read-only key is the right call. Which is roughly the whole thesis of [Stripe Revenue Tracker](https://example.com) — restricted-key only, the minimum scopes documented on the setup page, and the endpoints we call listed in the docs so you can check them against your Stripe logs. It's a small tool for small SaaS, and it doesn't ask for anything it doesn't need.

The broader point, though, isn't about any one vendor. It's that "read-only" is a spectrum, not a checkbox, and the vendors who understand that are the ones worth trusting with your customer list.
